refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed absolute.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, congestion profiles) agreed with customer. Measure across multiple teleoperation sessions and load scenarios. Acceptance: sustained packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) over a minimum observation window (e.g., 1 hour continuous operation).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction and/or retransmission strategies to minimize effective packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall provide network condition monitoring and graceful degradation when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall enforce operational constraints when teleoperation communication quality falls below safety-critical thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover and packet loss monitoring at the transport layer.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive codec and compression with forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission of critical command/feedback packets.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to isolate teleoperation traffic from background network load.
DP-D: Combine DP-A, DP-B, and DP-C in a layered architecture: redundancy for resilience, FEC/retransmission for recovery, QoS for prevention.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered packet loss mitigation strategy combining (1) redundant communication channels with automatic failover, (2) adaptive forward error correction and selective retransmission for critical teleoperation packets, and (3) QoS-based traffic prioritization to isolate teleoperation traffic from background network congestion.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation communication quality to network infrastructure design (must support dual paths and QoS tagging).
DP-D couples to safety services: safety constraints must be enforced when communication quality degrades below thresholds.
DP-D couples to operator interface: operator must be informed of communication status and any operational constraints imposed due to poor link quality.
coupling_check.notes: Couplings are acceptable and expected at this level. They are managed through explicit sub-FR decomposition and interface definitions (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). No hidden or unintended couplings detected.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or hybrid). Risk: If network conditions are poorly specified or highly variable, packet loss targets may be unachievable.
Assumption: Redundant communication paths are available (e.g., dual cellular carriers, wired + wireless). Risk: In remote worksites, redundancy may be unavailable or prohibitively expensive.

Assumption: FEC and retransmission overhead (latency, bandwidth) are acceptable for teleoperation use cases. Risk: If latency budget is tight (e.g., <100ms round-trip), FEC/retransmission may introduce unacceptable delays.

Assumption: Safety services can enforce operational constraints (e.g., speed reduction, autonomous halt) when communication quality degrades. Risk: Operator may resist constraints; safety policy and training must be clear.
Risk: Packet loss ≤1% is a statistical target; transient bursts may exceed it. Mitigation: Define observation window and acceptable variance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) redundancy strategy (e.g., dual 4G carriers, or 4G + satellite), (2) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and retransmission policy (e.g., critical packets only), (3) QoS mechanism (e.g., DSCP marking, traffic shaping), (4) failover latency target (e.g., <500ms), and (5) safety constraint triggers (e.g., packet loss >2% for 10 seconds triggers speed reduction). These details will inform IM assignment and sub-FR decomposition.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including packet loss detection, error correction, retransmission, QoS enforcement, and communication status reporting. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (to send commands and receive telemetry) and Safety Services (to report communication quality and receive operational constraints).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (e.g., move, rotate, lift) from the tele-operator console. Tasking packets are prioritized for low-latency delivery and are subject to FEC/retransmission.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, equipment status) to the tele-operator console. Telemetry is subject to adaptive compression and FEC to minimize packet loss impact on operator situational awareness.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints from Safety Services (e.g., speed limits, operational mode restrictions) when communication quality degrades. Transmits communication quality metrics (packet loss %, latency, jitter) to Safety Services for real-time monitoring and constraint enforcement.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages physical/logical network connections (dual carriers, failover logic, QoS tagging). Monitors link quality and triggers redundancy switching. Provides raw packet loss metrics to the Teleoperation module for FEC/retransmission decisions.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Packet loss mitigation is a subsystem concern, not a solution-level concern. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility and obscure ownership. Not recommended.

Vehicle module owns the physical equipment and its onboard systems (sensors, actuators, local control). Teleoperation communication is external to the vehicle; it is a bridge between the vehicle and the tele-operator. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple vehicle design to communication architecture, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is explicitly designed to own the remote operation pipeline, including communication quality, command/feedback processing, and operator interface. Packet loss mitigation is a core teleoperation concern. All sub-functions (FEC, retransmission, QoS, failover) are naturally contained within this module. Highly recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and constraint application. While Safety Services must monitor communication quality and enforce operational constraints, it does not own the communication pipeline itself. Assigning IM to Safety Services would blur the boundary between communication quality assurance and safety policy. Recommended as a consumer of communication metrics, not the owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural and optimal owner of packet loss mitigation. It owns the end-to-end communication pipeline, has direct visibility into packet loss metrics, and can implement FEC, retransmission, and QoS strategies without coupling to vehicle or safety subsystems. Safety Services will consume communication quality metrics via the SafetyAlert interface and enforce operational constraints as needed.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if it also owns network infrastructure (dual carriers, failover logic). Mitigation: Delegate network link management to a dedicated Network Link abstraction (already in known interfaces), which Teleoperation consumes.

Risk: If packet loss is caused by vehicle-side issues (e.g., onboard radio failure), Teleoperation module may not have visibility. Mitigation: Vehicle module must report link quality metrics to Teleoperation via Telemetry interface.

Risk: Safety Services may need to enforce constraints faster than Teleoperation can react. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert interface with low-latency, high-priority delivery (e.g., separate channel or interrupt-driven).

Risk: Operator may override communication-based constraints, creating safety hazard. Mitigation: Safety policy and training must be clear; Safety Services must have authority to halt operation if constraints are violated.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has clear ownership of: (1) packet loss detection and reporting, (2) FEC/retransmission logic, (3) QoS enforcement, (4) failover decision-making, and (5) communication status display to operator. Delegate Network Link management to a separate abstraction (physical/logical network layer) that Teleoperation consumes. Ensure SafetyAlert interface is low-latency and high-priority. Define clear handoff between Teleoperation (communication quality assurance) and Safety Services (constraint enforcement).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layered packet loss mitigation) directly and sufficiently addresses FR-NGHE-009 (teleoperation packet loss ≤1%). The DP specifies three complementary strategies (redundancy, FEC/retransmission, QoS) that together provide high confidence in meeting the acceptance criterion. No logical gaps or misalignments detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module must not own physical network infrastructure (carriers, routers). Mitigation: Delegate to Network Link abstraction.
Teleoperation module must not own vehicle-side communication hardware. Mitigation: Vehicle module reports link quality via Telemetry; Teleoperation consumes it.
Teleoperation module must not own safety policy. Mitigation: Safety Services owns policy; Teleoperation provides metrics via SafetyAlert.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the clear and optimal owner of DP-D. All sub-functions (FEC, retransmission, QoS, failover) are naturally contained within the teleoperation communication pipeline. Interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and non-invasive. No diffusion or ownership ambiguity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics (via SafetyAlert or diagnostic interface) for measurement. Acceptance criterion (≤1% packet loss) is measurable and testable.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, network monitoring, safety constraint enforcement) are allocated to Teleoperation and Safety Services with clear ownership boundaries. No ambiguity or overlap.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify redundancy strategy, FEC scheme, QoS mechanism, failover latency target, and safety constraint triggers (see DP recommended_revision).
Define SafetyAlert interface protocol: packet loss threshold triggers, constraint types (speed reduction, mode restriction, halt), and latency requirements.
Define Network Link abstraction: what metrics does it expose to Teleoperation? What failover logic does it implement?
Clarify Telemetry interface: does it include link quality metrics (latency, jitter, packet loss %) from vehicle-side sensors?
Document assumptions about network conditions (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, hybrid) and customer acceptance process for defining packet loss targets.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic; it requires decomposition into sub-FRs to address packet loss detection, error correction, network monitoring, and safety constraint enforcement. DP-D is sound and well-aligned with the refined FR. Teleoperation module is the clear and optimal owner. However, DP-D is complex and requires further refinement (redundancy strategy, FEC scheme, QoS mechanism, failover logic, safety triggers). Sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to allocate these details to specific design elements (e.g., FEC encoder, retransmission buffer, QoS classifier, failover controller, safety monitor). Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to achieve sufficient design detail for implementation.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR is clear and verifiable, but DP-D is a high-level strategy that must be broken down into concrete design decisions. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) are well-defined and ready for individual decomposition. Each sub-FR will map to a specific DP and IM assignment at the next level. No blocking issues or gate failures; proceeding to decomposition is the natural next step.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction and/or retransmission strategies to minimize effective packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall provide network condition monitoring and graceful degradation when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall enforce operational constraints when teleoperation communication quality falls below safety-critical thresholds.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or hybrid).
Redundant communication paths are available (e.g., dual cellular carriers, wired + wireless).
FEC and retransmission overhead (latency, bandwidth) are acceptable for teleoperation use cases.
Safety services can enforce operational constraints (e.g., speed reduction, autonomous halt) when communication quality degrades.
Packet loss ≤1% is a statistical target; transient bursts may exceed it, but sustained average must meet criterion.
Teleoperation module has access to packet loss metrics from Network Link and Vehicle modules.
Safety Services has authority to halt operation if communication-based constraints are violated.
open_risks: If network conditions are poorly specified or highly variable, packet loss targets may be unachievable.
In remote worksites, redundant communication paths may be unavailable or prohibitively expensive.
If latency budget is tight (<100ms round-trip), FEC/retransmission may introduce unacceptable delays.
Operator may resist communication-based constraints, creating safety hazard.
Packet loss may be caused by vehicle-side issues (e.g., onboard radio failure) not visible to Teleoperation module.
Safety Services may need to enforce constraints faster than Teleoperation can react.
Failover between redundant channels may introduce transient packet loss or latency spikes.
evidence_needed: Customer specification of agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G LTE coverage, WiFi 6 availability, hybrid strategy).
Customer acceptance criteria for packet loss measurement (observation window, acceptable variance, test scenarios).
Network infrastructure assessment: availability of redundant paths, QoS support, failover capabilities.
Latency budget analysis: end-to-end latency requirement for teleoperation (e.g., <200ms round-trip), impact of FEC/retransmission on latency.
Safety policy and operational constraints: what constraints are enforced when communication quality degrades? How are they communicated to operator?
Operator training and acceptance: operator understanding of communication-based constraints and willingness to comply.
Prototype or simulation results: packet loss mitigation effectiveness under representative network conditions.
